coredata.dc:title: Histidine-functionalized carbon-based dot-Zinc(II) nanoparticles as a novel stabilizer for Pickering emulsion synthesis of polystyrene microspheres
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science
coredata.dc:creator: Ruiyi, Li
Zaijun, Li
Junkang, Liu
coredata.dc:description: Carbon-based dots (CDs) are nanoparticles with size-dependent optical and electronic properties that have been widely applied in energy-efficient displays and lighting, photovoltaic devices and biological markers. However, conventional CDs are difficult to be used as ideal stabilizer for Pickering emulsion due to its irrational amphiphilic structure. The study designed and synthesized a new histidine-functionalized carbon dot-Zinc(II) nanoparticles, which is termed as His-CD-Zn. The His-CD was made via one-step hydrothermal treatment of histidine and maleic acid. The His-CD reacted with Zn2+ to form His-CD-Zn. The as-prepared His-CD-Zn was used as a solid particle surfactant for stabilizing styrene-in-water emulsion. The Pickering emulsion exhibits high stability and sensitive pH-switching behaviour. The introduction of S2O8 2− triggers the emulsion polymerization of styrene. The resulted polystyrene microsphere was well coated with His-CDs on the surface. It was successfully used as an ideal adsorbent for removal of heavy metallic ions from water with high adsorption capacity. The study also provides a prominent approach for fabrication of amphiphilic carbon-based nanoparticles for stabilizing Pickering emulsion.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Synthesis
Carbon dot
Pickering emulsion
Polystyrene microspheres

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Colloid and Interface Science

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: The Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The procedure for synthesis of His-CD-Zn.
TEM image (A) and FTIR spectrum (B) of His-CD.
PLS spectra of His-CD system after added 0.0, 0.002, 0.004, 0.008, 0.01 and 0.012M of Zn(II) (from bottom to top).
SEM image (A) of His-CD-Zn and zeta potential (B) of His-CD-Zn dispersion at different pH.
Optical microscopic image of the Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn.
Optical microscopic image of the Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn after S2O8 2− was added into the emulsion for 5min (A), 1h (B), 2h (C) and 5h (D).
The SEM image (A) of His-CD/PS and the EDX of carbon element (B), nitrogen element (C) and oxygen element (D) in the His-CD/PS.
(A) Adsorption capacities of the His-CD/PS towards different metallic ions. (B) The desorption rate of different metallic ions on the His-CD/PS surface.
The amount adsorbed for Pb2+ as a function of adsorption-desorption cycle. Adsorption conditions: Co: 200mgl−1, pH: 7.0, temperature: 25°C; equilibrium time: 10min.

head.ce:title: Histidine-functionalized carbon-based dot-Zinc(II) nanoparticles as a novel stabilizer for Pickering emulsion synthesis of polystyrene microspheres

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology, Ministry of Education, Wuxi 214122, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Food Colloids and Biotechnology

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author at: School of Chemical and Material Engineering, Jiangnan University, Wuxi 214122, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemical and Material Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The study reported a novel histidine-functionalized carbon-based dot-Zinc(II) nanoparticles (His-CD-Zn) as novel stabilizer for Pickering emulsion synthesis of polystyrene microspheres (PS). The as-prepared His-CD/PS have been successfully applied in adsorption of heavy metallic ions from water.

Carbon-based dots (CDs) are nanoparticles with size-dependent optical and electronic properties that have been widely applied in energy-efficient displays and lighting, photovoltaic devices and biological markers. However, conventional CDs are difficult to be used as ideal stabilizer for Pickering emulsion due to its irrational amphiphilic structure. The study designed and synthesized a new histidine-functionalized carbon dot-Zinc(II) nanoparticles, which is termed as His-CD-Zn. The His-CD was made via one-step hydrothermal treatment of histidine and maleic acid. The His-CD reacted with Zn2+ to form His-CD-Zn. The as-prepared His-CD-Zn was used as a solid particle surfactant for stabilizing styrene-in-water emulsion. The Pickering emulsion exhibits high stability and sensitive pH-switching behaviour. The introduction of S2O8 2− triggers the emulsion polymerization of styrene. The resulted polystyrene microsphere was well coated with His-CDs on the surface. It was successfully used as an ideal adsorbent for removal of heavy metallic ions from water with high adsorption capacity. The study also provides a prominent approach for fabrication of amphiphilic carbon-based nanoparticles for stabilizing Pickering emulsion.

ce:section.ce:para: Graphite oxide (GO) exists in the form several layers of graphene and its chemical composition is classified into the rich oxidized region and pool of un-oxidized graphitic domains. In the oxidized region hydrophilic functional groups such as epoxy and hydroxyl at the planar surface and carboxyl, carbonyl, ester, ether, diol, ketone, phenol, quinine, and lactones at the edges are anchored to sp3 carbon atoms. The graphitic domain consists of unperturbed hexagonal aromatic chains of sp2 bonded carbon atoms [1,2]. The unique quality inherent within GO structure enables the material to act as an effective stabilizer to isolate hydrophobic materials such as carbon nanotube and water-insoluble drugs [3]. The main advantage of having GO as surfactant to stabilize carbon allotropes lies on its chemical compound, which mostly contains conjugated carbon to carbon bonding, as well as the two-dimensional configuration, which serves to improve material functionalities [4]. Owing to the unique electrical, mechanical and chemical properties, GO as a stabilizer for Pickering emulsions have attracted a great interest [5]. The emulsion stabilized by GO has been successfully applied in fabrication of various GO-functionalized polymers such as polyaniline [6], polystyrene [7] and polymer [8], and nanoparticles such as Au [9], Ag [10], Fe2O3 [11] and ZIF-8 [12]. Many investigations confirmed that the introduction of GO can greatly improve the electrochemical, thermal and mechanical properties of the materials. However, GO as a surfactant exists two inherent deficiencies from its structure. The 2D graphene sheets of GO has a relatively large size from several micrometers to decades micrometers. The graphene sheets are easy to aggregation on the fluid interface, which increases the rigidity of interfacial layer and lead to a poor emulsion stability. In addition, GO offers a very strong hydrophobicity and a very weak hydrophilicity. The irrational amphiphilic structure makes it impossible to neatly arrange on the oil-water interface, leading to low surface activity. This greatly limits the applications of GO as a solid particle surfactant in many fields that need high emulsion stability.

Carbon-based dots (CDs), single- or few-layer graphene with a tiny size of only several nanometers, stand for a new type of quantum dots with the unique properties associated with both graphene and quantum dots [13]. CDs are particularly encouraging owing to their outstanding optical properties, low toxicity, good biocompatibility, and robust chemical inertness [14]. Thus, many interesting phenomena that are different from those in normal CDs are highly expected in view of the remarkable quantum-confinement and edge effects of 0D CD. Indeed, the extraordinary optoelectronic properties of CD have been revealed recently and their important potentials in solar cells [15], fuel cells [16], lithium ion batteries [17] and supercapacitors [18] have emerged. Compared with conventional GO, graphene sheets of CD give a much less size and more rich of hydrophilic groups [19]. The small size helps to effectively reduce the rigidity of the interfacial layer caused by aggregation of graphene sheets and neatly arrange on the oil-water interface, leading to an enhanced surface activity and emulsion stability. The rich of hydrophilic groups make CD offers a much better water-solubility compared with GO. Because of the irrational amphiphilic structure, conventional CD is difficult to be used as a stabilizer for Pickering emulsion. However, the unique structure of CD also leave us a huge design space to largely improve the amphiphilicity. By changing 2D graphene sheet into 3D nanoparticle and improving the hydrophobicity, CD is highly likely to be an ideal solid particle surfactant for stabilizing Pickering emulsion.

In the study, we focus on the synthesis of histidine-functionalized carbon-based dot-Zinc(II) nanoparticles (termed as His-CD-Zn) via the hydrothermal treatment of histidine and maleic acid and subsequent coordination reaction of the graphene sheets with Zn2+. The study reveals that the polymerization of graphene sheets achieves to synchronously change 2D graphene sheets into 3D nanoparticles and improve the hydrophobicity. The appropriate amphiphilic structure of His-CD-Zn makes it become an ideal solid particle surfactant for stabilizing Pickering emulsion. The styrene-in-water emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn exhibits high stability and sensitive pH-switching behaviour. It has been successfully applied in the fabrication of polystyrene microspheres.

Histidine (99+%) and maleic acid (99+%) were purchased from Sigma-Aldrich Chemical Company (Mainland, China). All other reagents employed were of analytical reagent grade or with the highest quality and were purchased from Shanghai Chemical Company (Shanghai, China). Ultra pure water (18.2MΩcm) purified from a Milli-Q purification system was used throughout the experiment.

His-CD was made by hydrothermal treatment of histidine and maleic acid [20]. In a typical synthesis, hisidine (2g) and maleic acid (4g) and were dissolved in ultra pure water (25ml). The mixed solution was evaporated at 80°C until dry, heated at 220°C for 2h and cooled to room temperature. The resulted His-CD sample was dissolved in ultra pure water to form a transparent His-CD solution (50mgml−1). The acidity of the solution was adjusted to pH 7 by sodium hydroxide and then dialyzed in a dialysis bag with a 3kDa cut-off molecular weight with change of water every 6h before the precipitate occurred in the bag. The collected solution in the bag was dried by free-drying to obtain His-CD solid sample.

The Zinc chloride aqueous solution (1ml, 0.1M) was slowly dropped into the as-prepared His-CD aqueous solution (10ml, 1.0mgml−1) under intense agitation. The obtained His-CD-Zn dispersion was stored in a refrigerator at 4°C before use.

The His-CD-Zn dispersion (1ml), styrene (10ml) and ultra pure water (10ml) were added into a centrifuge tube (50ml), respectively. Followed by stirring for 20s at 10,000rpm using a HN-30K handheld homogenizer (Shanghai HANUO Instrument Co. Ltd, China) to obtain Pickering emulsion.

The Pickering emulsion (20ml) was transferred into the flask. After added azobisisobutyronitrile (2mg) and FeSO4 (2mg), it was treated by stirring for 10s at 10,000rpm on the homogenizer. After the emulsion was placed for 10min, K2S2O8 (60mg) was added and emulsified ultrasonically for 15min. The flask was flushed with N2 during the reaction. The polymerization was conducted under at 80°C for 8h. After that, the hydrochloric acid solution (5M, 0.05ml) was added into the system. The collected precipitate was washed for 5 times with ultra pure water and dried at 80°C to obtain the His-CD-coated polystyrene microspheres (His-CD/PS).

Scanning electron microscope (SEM) and energy dispersive X-ray spectrum (EDX) analysis were performed in a HITACHI S4800 field emission scanning electron microscope. SEM samples were done by slowly dropping diluted ethanol dispersion of the products on a copper sheet attached to an aluminum sample holder, and the solvent need to be completely evaporated at room temperature. Transmission electron microscope (TEM) analysis was carried out on a JEOL 2010 (HR) transmission electron microscope at 200keV. The samples were prepared by dispersing a small amount of powder-like products into absolute ethanol. Then, one drop of the above suspension was dropped on the surface of 300mesh copper and was dried under a infra-red lamp for volatilizing ethanol. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were measured on a X-ray D8 Advance instrument operated at 40kV and 20mA and using Cu Kα radiation source with λ=0.15406nm. Infrared spectra (IR) were recorded on a Nicolet FT-IR 6700 spectrometer and Raman measurements were carried out using InVia laser micro-Raman spectrometer. UV–visible spectra were recorded on a TU-1901 spectrometer with a DH-2000 deuterium and tungsten halogen light source in absorbance mode (Beijing Purkinje General Instrument Co., Ltd, China). Fluorescence spectrum was recorded on the Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrophotometer (Varian, America). For the resonant light scattering (RLS) measurement, the excitation and emission wave-lengths were scanned simultaneously in the wavelength range from 200nm to 800nm with Δλ=0. The optical microscopy images of Pickering emulsion were recorded using a VHX-1000C Shooting optical microscope (Kean Co. Ltd, Hongkong in China).

The synthesis of His-CD-Zn includes tow processes (shown in Fig. 1 ). Firstly, His-CD was made via one-step hydrothermal treatment of histidine and maleic acid. Often, CD was prepared by the hydrothermal reaction of citric acid. The resulted CD always contains rich of oxygen-containing groups at the edge of graphene sheets such as hydroxyl, epoxide and carboxyl groups [21]. Owing to its excellent water-solubility, graphene sheets in the CD can be completely wetted by water. They will remain in a single water phase and a stable emulsion fails to form. Thus, significantly enhance the hydrophobicity is only approach to improve the amphiphilicity of CD. In the study, we designed new strategies for the fabrication of amphiphilic CD. One strategy is to use maleic acid instead of citric acid to form graphene sheets. Similar to citric acid, maleic acid can make the hexagonal sp2-bonded carbon network and finally lead to form graphene sheets during thermal hydrolysis via the condensation reaction between different molecules and subsequently carbonization. However, maleic acid doesn’t exist any hydrophilic groups unless carboxyl groups. As the most of Its carboxyl groups will be consumed during the thermal hydrolysis, the formed graphene sheet contains a much less hydrophilic groups compared to the graphene sheet obtained by hydrolysis of citric acid, indicating an enhanced hydrophobicity. Another strategy is to introduce imidazole ring into the edge of graphene sheets via a condensation between maleic acid and histidine. The nitrogen atom in the imidazole ring offers strong coordination ability to metallic ions, thus transition metallic ions will cause an obvious polymerization of graphene sheets through the coordination bonds, in which metallic ion acts as a linker. The polymerization greatly improves the hydrophobicity due to its much less polarity compared to single graphene sheet. More importantly, the CD may be designed as a typical amphiphilic particle by the polymerization of graphene sheets, which is close to classical organic surfactant in the amphiphilic structure.

The as-prepared His-CD was characterized by TEM and Fourier transform Infrared spectra (FTIR). Fig. 2 A presents typically TEM image of His-CD. It can be seen that the His-CD has approximately 2D sheet shape with the average particle size of 5.6nm. On the IR spectrum of His-CD in Fig. 2B there are many strong IR absorption peaks or bands. The peak at about 3420cm−1 is the symmetric stretching vibration IR absorption of NH. The peak at 1697cm−1 could be assigned to the CO stretch vibration. The peak at about 1382cm−1 could be attributed to CH stretching vibration, suggesting the presence of carbonyl and CH group. The Band at 3379cm−1 could correspond to the stretching vibrations of COOH and NH groups. The His-CD offers a more simple IR spectrum and weaker absorption intensities compared to single histidine and maleic acid. This is because most of the active groups in the maleic acid and histidine molecules have been consumed during their polymerization and carbonization. However, almost all characteristics IR absorption peaks of main functional groups in histidine and maleic acid can be also observed on the FTIR spectrum of His-CD, including NH, COOH and imidazole ring. The result demonstrates that imidazole ring has been successfully introduced into the edge of graphene sheets.

To investigate the optical property, the fluorescence excitation and emission spectra of His-CD were measured and the results were listed in Fig. s1. His-CD has a strong fluorescence emission with the maximum fluorescence peak at 440nm. Among maleic acid and histidine, maleic acid has no any fluorescence emission in the wavelength range from 200nm to 800nm under the conditions. Although histidine can produce the fluorescence emission due to the existence of imidazole ring. However, its fluorescence emission lies at the wavelength of less than 350nm with a low intensity. From Fig. s1, we noted that the fluorescence emission mainly lies at the wavelength of more than 350nm. The result verifies that the fluorescence emission could be attributed to the formed CD rather than pristine maleic acid and histidine, indicating the formation of His-CD again. In addition, the relative fluorescence quantum yield of His-CD was also measured using quinine sulfate as a standard. The quantum yield of His-CD is about 52.6%, which is more than that of the CD prepared by thermal hydrolysis of single maleic acid (22.7%). The fact reveals that the introduction of imidazole ring can enhance the fluorescence emission.

The His-CD reacted with Zn2+ to rapidly form His-CD-Zn. For the His-CD, its nitrogen atoms in the imidazole ring can react with Zn2+ to form stable compound via the coordination bonds between the nitrogen atom and Zn2+. The reaction may occur in the interior of a graphene sheet and between different graphene sheets. The former doesn’t bring the increase of the particle size but lead to reduce of the polarity of His-CD due to the formation of coordination bonds, indicating an enhanced hydrophobicity. The latter results in the polymerization of graphene sheets and forms bigger solid particles, leading to an improved the hydrophobicity. With the increase of Zn2+ content in the reaction system the degree of the polymerization of graphene sheets will rapidly increase. At the same time, the reaction was accompanied by the decrease in the hydrophilicity and the increase in the hydrophobicity. Based on the theory of coordination chemistry, one Zn2+ can combine with four nitrogen atoms to form tetrahedral complex. As a result, growth of the complex is multi-faceted during the polymerization of graphene sheets, and finally leads to the formation of quasi spherical particles. Compared to 2D graphene sheets, the quasi spherical particles are much easier to assemble at the fluid interface and results in an reduced interfacial tension. Thus, 3D His-CD-Zn nanoparticles are more suitable to stabilize oil-in-water emulsion. To confirm the above suggest, the effect of Zn2+ content in the system on the formation of His-CD-Zn was investigated in the laboratory. The system is transparent and no any precipitate can be observed when the concentration of Zn2+ is less than 0.02M (shown in Fig. s2). This is because the reaction between Zn2+ and nitrogen atom mainly occurs in the interior of a graphene sheet. The formed complex has the same size as the pristine graphene sheets, indicating an excellent water-solubility. At the same time, the above reaction may partly take place in between different graphene sheets, leading to the polymerization of a small number of graphene sheets. As the formed complex in a very low concentration of Zn2+ has a low degree of polymerization, its particles also show a high water-solubility. So, no precipitation can be observed in the system. However, the system began to become turbid when the concentration of Zn2+ reach to 0.02M. Continue to increase the concentration of Zn2+, the amounts of precipitation rapidly increases. This is because the reaction of Zn2+ with the nitrogen atoms mainly occurs in between graphene sheets. The degree of the polymerization of graphene sheets rapidly increases with the increase of Zn2+ content, resulting in an enhanced hydrophobicity and reduced water-solubility. The formed polymer will be precipitated from water due to its lower water-solubility. In addition, the reaction between the His-CD and Zn2+ was also monitored by the RLS measurement in the absence and presence of Zn2+. As shown in Fig. 3 , the addition of Zn2+ cannot change shape of the RLS spectrum, but it can result in an obvious increase in the RLS intensity. The intensity will increase with the increase of Zn2+, verifying that the reaction of Zn2+ with His-CD take places in between graphene sheets. Owing to the polymerization of graphene sheets, the size of His-CD-Zn particles is bigger than that of the pristine graphene sheets in the His-CD, leading to an enhanced PLS intensity. The particle size will further increase with increasing the Zn2+ content. This may cause large amounts of the precipitation and lead to further increase in the RLS intensity.

SEM image of the as-prepared His-CD-Zn was shown in Fig. 4 A. The His-CD-Zn consists of 3D nanoparticles with the average particle size of 46nm. The size is much bigger than that of pristine His-CDs (5.6nm). The result verifies that the polymerization of graphene sheets achieves to the conversion of 2D graphene sheets into 3D nanoparticles. During the polymerization, several nanometers of graphene sheets constitute well-defined 3D nanomaterials. However, Its big size and steric hindrance lead to a poor crystallization kinetics process. This greatly restricts the growth and arrangement of crystals and leads to the formation of irregular nanocrystals, which has been well confirmed by the XRD analysis. On the XRD pattern of His-CD-Zn in Fig. s3 there is a weak diffraction peak at 24°, corresponding to the crystal plane (002) of graphene. The pattern is similar with that of His-CD, verifying that the polymerization does not produce a new crystal surface. To test the effect of pH of the medium on the amphiphilicty, the zeta potentials of His-CD-Zn were measured at different pH in the range of 6–14. Fig. 4B indicates that the His-CD-Zn offers a negative zeta potential at total pH range from pH 6 to 14, indicating negative charges attached on the surface of His-CD-Zn. The zeta potential rapidly increases with the increase of pH, indicating more negative charges attached on the surface of His-CD-Zn. The negative zeta potential could be attributed to the dissociation of carboxyl groups in the His-CD-Zn at a relatively high pH. The existence of more negative charges will improve stability of the His-CD-Zn dispersion owing to their strong electrostatic repulsion. Since the hydrophilicity and hydrophobicity of His-CD depends on the charges on the surface, the amphiphilic properties of His-CD-Zn can be easily adjusted by changing pH of the medium.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn
ce:section.ce:para: The His-CD-Zn was used as a novel solid surfactant for stabilizing styrene-in-water emulsion. Optical microscopic image of the Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn nanoparticles was presented in Fig. 5 . All droplets in the Pickering emulsion are regular microspheres with a relatively narrow size distribution. As the His-CD-Zn nanoparticles are more hydrophilic particles, these tend to stable oil-in-water emulsion. The solid particles were assembled at the styrene/water interface, because of the reduced total interfacial energy that occurs when part of liquid-liquid interface is replaced with liquid-particle interface. The styrene was covered by the solid particles and lies at center of the droplets. Compared to conventional graphene and graphene oxide, the His-CD-Zn nanoparticles offer a much smaller size, 3D structure and more reasonable amphiphilic structure. These advantages create a spherical shape of the droplet with a smooth surface and good stability of the Pickering emulsion.

His-CD can react with Zn2+ to form a stable His-CD-Zn complex at a neutral or alkaline medium, which indicates high surface activity. If the acidity of the medium was adjusted to pH of less than 5 by the addition of strong acid, the formed complex will be rapidly decomposed into free graphene sheets and Zn2+, which only can offer a very low surface activity. Therefore, the Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn seriously depends on the acidity of the medium. To study on the response of the Pickering emulsion to pH of the medium, the Pickering emulsion was made in the neutral medium using the same procedure described in Section 2.3, indicating a “on” state of the Pickering emulsion. The formed emulsion was destroyed by the addition of the HCI aqueous solution (5M, 100μl) into the above system, indicating a “off” state of the Pickering emulsion. After that, the Pickering emulsion was recovered by the addition of the NaOH aqueous solution (5M, 50μl), indicating a “on” state of the Pickering emulsion again. The above procedure was repeated for 20 times. The result shows that the emulsion can be well recovered by the addition of NaOH after each of the “off” with HCI, indicating an excellent pH-switching behaviour.

To achieve the emulsion polymerization of styrene, the amounts of S2O8 2− was introduced into the Pickering emulsion stabilized by His-CD-Zn under the intense agitation. Fig. 6 shows that the introduction of S2O8 2− rapidly triggers the emulsion polymerization of styrene to great number of small polystyrene microspheres (PS). With the increase of the reaction time, the size of the PS obviously increases. The growth of the microspheres almost stopped when the time reaches to 8h. To obtain high productivity, the reaction of 8h was employed for the preparation of PS in the following work. The collected PS was washed by the addition of 100μl of 5M HCI solution to remove the Zn2+ in the His-CD-Zn nanoparticles. The His-CD has a relatively high hydrophilicity and a relatively weak hydrophobicity. The irrational amphiphilic structure make it only offers a poor surface activity. For the above reason, a rapid phase separation process was observed after added the HCI aqueous solution. During the process, Zn2+ was removed from the surface of PS. The resulted PS was separated from the system and deposited on the bottom of the vial.

The as-prepared PS was characterized by SEM and EDS technologies. Fig. 7 shows that the PS is regular spheres with a narrow size distribution from 2μm to 3μm in diameter were observed in Fig. 7A. The EDX analysis proves that the PS is mainly composed of carbon, nitrogen and oxygen elements. Among these elements, carbon element could be attributed to His-CD and PS. Nitrogen and oxygen elements could be attributed to His-CD. The result confirms that the His-CD is made up of His-CD and PS. In addition, EDX images in Fig. 7C and D indicate that nitrogen and oxygen element offer an excellent dispersion on the surface of PS. The excellent dispersion of nitrogen and oxygen elements demonstrate that His-CD have been well coated on the surface of PS.

To test the feasibility of His-CD/PS for possible application in the adsorption of heavy metallic ions, the amounts of Pb2+, Cd2+, Zn2+, Ni2+ or Cu2+ standard solution was transferred into a beaker. Then, added His-CD/PS into the above solution and then stirred on the magnetic mixer at 160rpm under room temperature for a given time. For each of these metallic ions, the adsorption capacity (AC) (mgg−1) was calculated using Eq. (1):
(1)
AC =
(
C
0
-
C
e
) × V
m
where Co and Ce are initial and equilibrium concentration of metallic ion (mgl−1), determined by atomic absorption spectrometry. V is volume of the solution and m is the weight of His-CD/PS. In the work, the AC was investigated with a series of the metallic ion standard solutions with the concentration from 100 to 1000mgl−1. The results in Fig. 8 A show that the His-CD/PS exhibits high adsorption capacity for all metallic ions studied. This could be attributed to strong affinity of the His-CD on the PS surface with metallic ions via the coordination bonds. In addition, the HCI solution was used to desorb the metallic ions loaded on the His-CD/PS. Fig. 8B shows that all desorption rate are more than 98%, indicating that the metallic ions are easily desorbed from the PS surface.

The reusability of His-CD/PS for the adsorption of metallic ion was evaluated via ten consecutive adsorption-desorption. During the process, the metallic ion loaded His-CD/PS was desorbed with 0.1M of HCI solution. The obtained His-CD/Ps was washed to neutral with ultra pure water and then used for the next adsorption of metallic ion. Fig. 9 presents the adsorption capacity of Pb2+ for ten consecutive adsorption-desorption. It can be seen that the His-CD/PS always kept good adsorptive property towards Pb2+, indicating an excellent reusability.

The study demonstrates synthesis of histidine-functionalized carbon-based dots via one-step hydrothermal treatment of histidine and maleic acid. The nanosize graphene sheets were polymerized into carbon dots-Zinc nanoparticles, in which Zinc ion acts as a linker between graphene sheets via the coordination bonds. The Pickering emulsion stabilized by the nanoparticles exhibits high stability and excellent pH-switching behaviour. It has been successfully applied in the synthesis of polystyrene microspheres. The study also provides a prominent approach for the fabrication of amphiphilic carbon-based nanoparticles for stabilizing Pickering emulsion.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors acknowledge the financial support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 21576115) and the Prospective Joint Research Project: Cooperative Innovation Fund (No. BY2015019-26).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found, in the online version, at http://dx.doi.org/10.1016/j.jcis.2017.01.018.
